claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period; Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions.

First, let me read the official guidance to ensure I provide you with accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. Now let me help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To start, could you please tell me: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to know if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. I have one child - my son Alex, he's 17.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask you a few questions.

Since Alex is 17, an important factor is whether he's in approved education or training. **Is Alex currently in approved education or training** (such as full-time non-advanced education, A-levels, Scottish Highers, or an approved training course)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not. He finished school and he's not doing any courses or training at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Let me ask you: **Has Alex started paid work for 24 hours or more a week, or is he receiving any benefits in his own right** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he's working full-time now - more than 24 hours a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me make my assessment based on the rules:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why: While you can normally continue to receive Child Benefit for children aged 16-19 if they remain in approved education or training, the rules state that you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training.

Since Alex is 17, has finished school, is not in any approved education or training, and is working full-time (more than 24 hours a week), you do not qualify for Child Benefit for him.

If Alex's circumstances change in the future - for example, if he decides to return to education or training - you may wish to check your eligibility again at that time.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old, which means he is over 16. According to Child Benefit rules, you normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. However, Alex is not in approved education or training. Additionally, the rules state that you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Since Alex is working full-time (more than 24 hours a week) and is not in approved education or training, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 17 years old. Alex has left education, is not in any approved training, and is working full-time (more than 24 hours per week). Under Child Benefit rules, eligibility ceases when a child over 16 starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.

According to Child Benefit rules, you normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. However, Alex is not in approved education or training. Additionally, the rules state that you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Since Alex is working full-time (more than 24 hours a week) and is not in approved education or training, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 17 years old. Alex has left education, is not in any approved training, and is working full-time (more than 24 hours per week). Under Child Benefit rules, eligibility ceases when a child over 16 starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.
